LinkedIn respects your privacy

LinkedIn and 3rd parties use essential and non-essential cookies to provide, secure, analyze and improve our Services, and to show you relevant ads (including professional and job ads) on and off LinkedIn. Learn more in our Cookie Policy.

Select Accept to consent or Reject to decline non-essential cookies for this use. You can update your choices at any time in your settings.

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Sign in Join now
Loveena Kamath
1y
  • Report this post

LinkedIn’s Easy apply button is the hardest way to get a job. Look, the easier the application process, the more the number of applicants. So, if you find an interesting job profile on LinkedIn, copy its title—and if the job ID is available, copy that too. Next, go to the same company's career page, paste the title, and try to find the exact role. Once you apply, go back to LinkedIn, find 8-10 people from the same company, who are from junior to mid level, and message them the link along with your CV and why you are fit for that particular role. If your request is specific and your LinkedIn profile looks genuine, then the employee will definitely help you since they also earn a referral bonus for successful hires. Sample message you can use to request referrals: Hi, Hope you are doing well I am _ and I came across this opportunity at | company name| for the role of (role name) My skills exactly align with the job requirements and would be great if you can take out some time to refer me for the same. Attached is my resume for your reference. Here is the link to the job role for your convenience: Happy to share any further detail you may require. Thanks & Regards,

2,702 53 Comments
Like Comment
Transcript
LinkedIn Easy Apply button is the hardest way to get a job look. The easier the application process, the more the number of applicants. So if you find an interesting job profile on LinkedIn, copy its title and if the job ID is available, copy that too. Next go to the same companies career page, paste the title and try to find the exact role. Once you apply, go back to LinkedIn, find 10 to 15 people from the same company who are from junior to mid level and message them the link along with your CV and why you are fit. For that particular role, if your request is specific and your LinkedIn profile looks genuine, then the employee will definitely help you since they will also earn a referral bonus for successful hires. Check the description for a sample message you can use to request referrals and follow Full disclosure for more.
Akhil Raj TS 1y
  • Report this comment

Please dont apply for a job by registering in the company website yourself if you are going to ask for a referral - Only apply for the job through the referral link provided by the person inside the company.

Like
Reply
13 Reactions 14 Reactions
J R. 1y
  • Report this comment

This is a great approach, Loveena! I completely agree that navigating the job application process can be challenging, especially with the volume of applicants utilizing the Easy Apply feature. Researching the company and reaching out for referrals not only shows initiative but also helps to build connections within the organization. I would also suggest tailoring your resume to highlight specific skills and experiences that align closely with the job description. Additionally, engaging with the company’s content on LinkedIn and participating in discussions can help you get noticed by potential connections before even reaching out. Has anyone here tried this method before? What was your experience like?

Like
Reply
5 Reactions 6 Reactions
Krishnakanth Reddy Mula, GMS-T 1y
  • Report this comment

FYI : in most of the HR systems; Once you apply through the company portal, your profile is marked as “already existing,” making the referring employee ineligible for the bonus. However, it’s still a good idea to connect with existing employees to see if anyone is willing to help!

Like
Reply
6 Reactions 7 Reactions

M NU 1y
  • Report this comment

Hi Loveena, The information you provided is useful, but I believe this type of referral can sometimes be misused among friends. I recommend reaching out directly to the hiring team for this position, as they are the ones who posted it and will have the most accurate information available on their website. This is just my perspective; it’s not meant to contradict your advice, but I hope others might agree to some extent. Add emojis

Like
Reply
3 Reactions 4 Reactions
Wayne Martin Jr 1y
  • Report this comment

Wanna know what’s so cumbersome about this process? There is an easy apply, but then we have to login to their site, create a whole new user account, then upload the CSV, then go ahead and manually type out everything from that same CSV. These companies don’t want to hire, they want to get you tired of writing everything that you have in your CSV.

More Relevant Posts

  • Apporva Arya
    9mo
    • Report this post

    🔎 Job Applications – What Should Be the Right Strategy? In today’s market, just applying everywhere is not enough. A thoughtful approach can make all the difference: ✅ Quality over Quantity – Instead of sending 100 random applications, focus on 10 tailored ones. ✅ Customize Your Resume – Highlight skills that align with the job description. ✅ Leverage Your Network – Referrals often carry more weight than cold applications. ✅ Prepare, Don’t Just Apply – Sharpen interview skills, revise fundamentals, and be ready when the call comes. ✅ Track & Reflect – Keep a log of applications and learn from rejections. 💡 Applying for jobs is not just about reaching out, it’s about standing out. What’s your strategy while applying for new opportunities? 🚀

    49 1 Comment
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Gemma Appleford
    9mo
    • Report this post

    After last week’s tip about checking your contact details on your application, here’s another small but important one for entry-level job seekers! 🔍 Tip #2: Always check your junk/spam mailbox! I’ve lost count of the number of replies I’ve received weeks, sometimes months, later saying “I completely missed this as it went into my junk mailbox!” It might sound obvious, but you’d be surprised how often important emails end up in the junk folder. Make it a habit to check your junk folder regularly, especially if you’re actively applying for roles. You can also add key contacts to your safe sender list to help avoid this. More tips coming soon! Follow along if you're applying for early careers roles or supporting someone who is ☺️ #EarlyCareers #JobSearchTips #CareerAdvice #JobHunting #RecruitmentTips #EntryLevelJobs

    17
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Divya Porwal
    9mo
    • Report this post

    Apply, apply, apply and apply…. as much as possible. Do not wait for the perfect time. I read somewhere: “Do not apply from Sept to Jan, companies do not hire then.” This may be true for senior roles since many people switch after year-end hikes. But the reality is: 👉 If you are not happy in your current company, there is no right time 👉 Once you start getting interviews, the pressure will be different. Things that normally take months to learn will take days Lock-in does not mean no apply. Start today. Tips to apply smartly: • Apply on company career pages directly, not only job portals • Use LinkedIn filters (past 24 hours / last week) to find new jobs • Cold email or DM recruiters with a short pitch and resume • Ask for referrals politely from seniors or ex colleagues • Keep a tracker (Google Sheet or Notion) of all applications • Do not wait to master everything before applying. Start and learn in parallel Opportunities do not come when you are ready. They come when you start trying.

    703 70 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Lis Harris
    8mo Edited
    • Report this post

    Tired of applying to the same jobs on LinkedIn like everyone else? I got you 👇🏾 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗷𝗼𝗯𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝘆 𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 Many companies (especially smaller ones) don’t post jobs on LinkedIn because it costs companies a lot of money. Instead hiring teams announce job openings in LinkedIn posts to: - Get better quality candidates through their network. - Save money 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲’𝘀 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗵𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗻 𝗷𝗼𝗯𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻 (+ a video guide to help) 1️⃣ Type ‘[ROLE] AND Hiring’ in the LinkedIn search bar Add quotes around multi word jobs to ensure the search finds these words together. Examples: "Customer Success Manager" AND Hiring, Recruiter AND Hiring, "Project Manager" AND Hiring 2️⃣ Filter "Posts" and “Past Week” or "Past Month" - you can even add from “My Network” ✨ You'll see who is hiring so you can reach out to them directly. ✨ Plus jobs within your network allow for a friendly introduction. 3️⃣ Apply to the role This role is unlikely to be posted on LinkedIn which means fewer applications and you have the benefit of knowing exactly who is hiring 😉 4️⃣ Send a personal note to the poster using this template: Hi, [X]. I’m interested in the [Y] role you posted. My experience in [A] and [B] aligns with what you’re looking for and I can provide value in [C] way. My resume is attached. When would you be free for a chat as a next step? 🧠 And now you know how to unlock hidden jobs on LinkedIn. Time to search and let me know what you find! ------- 👋🏽 Hi I’m Lis and I help job seekers stand out and get hired at lisharriscoaching.com Like getting job search tips? Give this post a 👍🏽 ♻ Share this to help job seekers find a new role

    426 32 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Michael Boyo-Obayuwana
    8mo
    • Report this post

    Excellent post Lis Harris! Thank you for sharing such a practical yet often overlooked strategy. Many job seekers limit themselves to the formal “Jobs” tab, missing out on hidden opportunities posted organically by hiring teams. This approach not only expands visibility but also encourages proactive networking - key to uncovering roles that never make it to job boards. A true reminder that effective job search today is as much about strategic connection as it is about application.

    Lis Harris

    Helping you get hired ✨ Recruiting Leader ✨ Career Coach

    8mo Edited

    Tired of applying to the same jobs on LinkedIn like everyone else? I got you 👇🏾 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗯𝗲𝘀𝘁 𝗽𝗹𝗮𝗰𝗲 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗷𝗼𝗯𝘀 𝗶𝘀 𝗯𝘆 𝘀𝗲𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁𝘀 Many companies (especially smaller ones) don’t post jobs on LinkedIn because it costs companies a lot of money. Instead hiring teams announce job openings in LinkedIn posts to: - Get better quality candidates through their network. - Save money 𝗛𝗲𝗿𝗲’𝘀 𝗵𝗼𝘄 𝘁𝗼 𝗳𝗶𝗻𝗱 𝗵𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗲𝗻 𝗷𝗼𝗯𝘀 𝗼𝗻 𝗟𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗲𝗱𝗜𝗻 (+ a video guide to help) 1️⃣ Type ‘[ROLE] AND Hiring’ in the LinkedIn search bar Add quotes around multi word jobs to ensure the search finds these words together. Examples: "Customer Success Manager" AND Hiring, Recruiter AND Hiring, "Project Manager" AND Hiring 2️⃣ Filter "Posts" and “Past Week” or "Past Month" - you can even add from “My Network” ✨ You'll see who is hiring so you can reach out to them directly. ✨ Plus jobs within your network allow for a friendly introduction. 3️⃣ Apply to the role This role is unlikely to be posted on LinkedIn which means fewer applications and you have the benefit of knowing exactly who is hiring 😉 4️⃣ Send a personal note to the poster using this template: Hi, [X]. I’m interested in the [Y] role you posted. My experience in [A] and [B] aligns with what you’re looking for and I can provide value in [C] way. My resume is attached. When would you be free for a chat as a next step? 🧠 And now you know how to unlock hidden jobs on LinkedIn. Time to search and let me know what you find! ------- 👋🏽 Hi I’m Lis and I help job seekers stand out and get hired at lisharriscoaching.com Like getting job search tips? Give this post a 👍🏽 ♻ Share this to help job seekers find a new role

    1
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Krista Morris, CARW, CCSC
    9mo
    • Report this post

    A connection on LinkedIn recently told me they’d applied to over 200 jobs, with only two interviews. And guess where they’d been applying? LinkedIn. Indeed. ZipRecruiter. (Spoiler: they’re not the top performers anymore.) According to new data from Q2 2025 via Huntr and the indomitable Sam Wright, Google has the highest job application response rate at 9.3%, more than double LinkedIn’s 3.3%. That’s not a fluke. When you search directly on Google.com for “[Job Title] jobs near me,” you’re pulling listings from all over the web ➡️ employer sites, job boards, and company career pages that may not be posted anywhere else. That means less competition and fresher leads. If your job search is stuck, try changing where you look. Sometimes the how matters just as much as the what. Strategy over spray-and-pray. Job search smarter, not harder.

    133 23 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Terica Johnson
    9mo
    • Report this post

    Are Y’all Even Reading the Job Descriptions? 👀 I just came across a job posting with no title, no details, just filler text like: “[Insert day-to-day tasks here]” “[Specify technical skills required]” And it had 12 applicants. 😭 Like… y’all didn’t even know what job you were applying for. 💡 This is why I say: slow down and actually read before you click “Easy Apply.” Not every posting is real. Not every listing is complete. If you’re not intentional, you’ll waste time applying to roles that don’t even exist (or worse, aren’t legit). 👉🏾 Strategy > Speed. Read. Research. Then apply. #JobSearchTips #ReverseRecruiter #CareerAdvice #JobSeekers #CareerStrategy

    18 3 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • Shreya Mehta 🚀
    8mo
    • Report this post

    An average job seeker in the U.S. spends over 100 hours a month applying for jobs. That’s over 50 full days a year gone into job searching. In 2025, finding a good role is hard enough. Then you have to tweak your resume, write custom answers, and fill out endless forms over and over again. Job searching has quietly become a full-time job. But it doesn’t have to be that way when you use the right strategy + tools. A job seeker I know recently told me she was spending every evening and weekend applying for relevant roles, and still getting ghosted. Not because she wasn’t qualified, but because she didn’t have a real system. That’s when she started using JobCopilot, and here’s what changed: → JobCopilot found relevant roles across 500,000+ sites → It customized her resume based on the role to help her stand out → It auto-filled all the repetitive questions and forms → The tool applied on her behalf while she worked or rested → It sent a daily email summarizing everything (so she knows what's going on) → She ran separate searches for different roles that matched her expertise → JobCopilot got better over time based on her preferences Instead of chasing the process, she finally had space to focus on the things that actually move the needle: networking, prep, and showing up strong when it counted. JobCopilot didn’t get her the job. But it gave her time, consistency, and a way to stay in the game. If you’re feeling buried under applications, this might be worth trying. Save hours every week and land more interviews - try JobCopilot here: https://lnkd.in/gRUPDQrF

    186 29 Comments
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in

  • CoreTactic Career and Business Coaching

    1,862 followers

    8mo
    • Report this post

    Are you sitting on your couch right now 🛋️, clicking "apply" for the 47th time today? Let me guess - your application status looks like a ghost town 👻, with tumbleweeds rolling through your inbox. We've all been there. Applying to 50, 100, even hundreds of job postings only to hear... nothing. It's not just frustrating - it's a broken system ⚠️. There's a better way: Backchanneling. ✨ What is backchanneling? Simply put, it's getting a referral into a company instead of cold-applying through an applicant tracking system 🤝. Why does this work? 🧠 ⭐ Referred candidates are 15x more likely to be hired 📊 85% of jobs are filled through connections and networking, not job boards 🔍 Hiring managers trust recommendations from people they know Think about it: would you rather be one resume in a stack of 300+ applications, or would you rather be "Sarah's friend who has exactly the experience we need"? 🤔 Stop applying for jobs. Start searching for referrals. 🚫➡️🔎 This means: 🌐 Leveraging your LinkedIn connections (even 2nd and 3rd degree) 🎓 Reaching out to alumni from your school 🤝 Attending industry events and meetups ☕ Having coffee chats with people in your target companies I've coached thousands of professionals through career transitions, and I can tell you with certainty: your next job will likely come through a conversation 💬, not an application. Let's connect if you're struggling with your job.

    1
    Like Comment

    To view or add a comment, sign in
